level-1-rbac-permission.description: A pod that regularly lists running pods in the namespace is crashing immediately after startup. The pod itself and its ServiceAccount both exist, but the logs reveal a permissions error. Something is missing in the authorization chain between the ServiceAccount and the Kubernetes API.
level-1-rbac-permission.hint1: Start by reading the pod's logs to see the exact error message. Run: `kubectl logs pod-lister -n k8smissions`
level-1-rbac-permission.hint2: You should see something like 'User cannot list resource pods'. Now check which RBAC objects exist for the ServiceAccount being used: `kubectl get role,rolebinding -n k8smissions`
level-1-rbac-permission.hint3: The pod references a ServiceAccount, but that ServiceAccount has no Role granting it any permissions and no RoleBinding connecting it to one. Both resources are entirely absent from the manifest.
level-1-rbac-permission.reveal:
## What went wrong

In Kubernetes, a ServiceAccount has **zero permissions by default**. The pod-reader ServiceAccount was created, but no one ever granted it the right to call the pods API. Every request the pod makes is rejected with HTTP 403 Forbidden.

## Fix

Add a Role that allows reading pods, then bind it to the ServiceAccount:

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader-role
  namespace: k8smissions
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: pod-reader-binding
  namespace: k8smissions
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: pod-reader-role
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: pod-reader
  namespace: k8smissions
```

## Why this matters

This is the core of Kubernetes RBAC: the **Role** defines *what* is allowed (verbs on resources), and the **RoleBinding** says *who* gets those permissions. Neither alone is enough. The principle of least privilege means granting only `get`, `list`, `watch` — not `create`, `delete`, or `patch` — when read-only access is all that's needed.

level-2-securitycontext.description: A web pod fails to start and shows CreateContainerConfigError in its status. The manifest already has a security policy telling Kubernetes the container must not run as root. But despite that declaration, the pod is still being rejected at startup — because the policy is incomplete.
level-2-securitycontext.hint1: Look at the pod's events to get the exact rejection message: `kubectl describe pod web-app -n k8smissions`
level-2-securitycontext.hint2: The events should say 'container has runAsNonRoot and image will run as root'. The manifest enforces non-root but never specifies *which* non-root user ID to use, so Kubernetes falls back to the image's default — which is UID 0.
level-2-securitycontext.hint3: The `securityContext` block has `runAsNonRoot: true` but is missing the `runAsUser` field. Without an explicit UID, Kubernetes cannot satisfy the constraint and rejects the pod.
level-2-securitycontext.reveal:
## What went wrong

`runAsNonRoot: true` tells Kubernetes to enforce that the container does not start as root — but it does not tell Kubernetes *what* user to run as. The nginx image defaults to UID 0. Kubernetes detects this conflict and refuses to start the container.

## Fix

Add `runAsUser` with a non-zero UID and harden the container further:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
    securityContext:
      runAsNonRoot: true
      runAsUser: 1000
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL
```

Note: switch to `nginx-unprivileged` and port 8080, since non-root processes cannot bind to ports below 1024.

## Why this matters

Running containers as root means a process escape gives an attacker root on the host. `runAsNonRoot` paired with an explicit `runAsUser` is a two-step defense: Kubernetes rejects root at admission, and the UID pins the runtime identity. `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` closes the setuid door even if someone does get code execution.

level-3-resourcequota.description: A pod has been sitting in Pending for several minutes, but `kubectl get nodes` shows the cluster has available capacity. There is a ResourceQuota on this namespace and the pod is making a resource request that exceeds what the quota allows in total.
level-3-resourcequota.hint1: Look at the pending pod's events for the quota violation message: `kubectl describe pod resource-hungry-app -n k8smissions`
level-3-resourcequota.hint2: The events should say 'exceeded quota: compute-quota, requested: requests.cpu=2500m'. Now check what the namespace quota allows in total: `kubectl describe resourcequota compute-quota -n k8smissions`
level-3-resourcequota.hint3: The pod's `resources.requests.cpu` field is set to 2500m, but the namespace's `requests.cpu` hard limit is only 2000m. The pod requests more CPU than the entire namespace is allowed.
level-3-resourcequota.reveal:
## What went wrong

The ResourceQuota caps the namespace at 2 CPUs total (`requests.cpu: "2"`). The pod asks for 2500m (2.5 CPUs) — 25% more than the whole namespace is allowed. Kubernetes never even tries to find a node; the quota check fails at the API server before scheduling begins.

## Fix

Reduce the pod's resource requests to fit within the quota:

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "1"
    memory: "1Gi"
```

## Why this matters

ResourceQuotas are a namespace-level governance tool: they prevent any single team or workload from starving others. When a pod's requests exceed the quota, it is rejected at admission — `kubectl apply` succeeds but the pod never schedules. Always check `kubectl describe resourcequota` before setting large resource requests, and size requests to what the application actually needs, not its theoretical maximum.

level-4-networkpolicy-security.description: After the security team applied a new NetworkPolicy, the backend service stopped reaching the database. Both pods are running, the Service exists, and DNS resolves correctly — but every connection attempt times out. The policy that was applied is too broad.
level-4-networkpolicy-security.hint1: Check the backend container's output to confirm it's failing to connect: `kubectl logs backend -n k8smissions`
level-4-networkpolicy-security.hint2: List the NetworkPolicies and read their specs: `kubectl get networkpolicy -n k8smissions -o yaml`. Look for a policy that selects all pods (`podSelector: {}`) but has `policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]` with no actual rule blocks.
level-4-networkpolicy-security.hint3: The `deny-all` NetworkPolicy applies to every pod in the namespace (empty podSelector) and lists both Ingress and Egress as policy types, but defines no `ingress:` or `egress:` rules — which in Kubernetes means *deny everything*.
level-4-networkpolicy-security.reveal:
## What went wrong

A NetworkPolicy with `policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]` and no rules is not a no-op — it is an explicit deny-all. Once it applied to all pods, every packet was dropped: backend could not reach database, and DNS stopped working too.

## Fix

Replace the deny-all with targeted rules:

```yaml
# Allow database to receive from backend
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-database-ingress
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: database
  policyTypes: [Ingress]
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: backend
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 5432
---
# Allow backend to reach database and resolve DNS
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-backend-egress
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  policyTypes: [Egress]
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: database
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 5432
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
```

## Why this matters

NetworkPolicies are additive and default-deny once any policy selects a pod. Always pair a default-deny with explicit allow rules, and remember that DNS (port 53 UDP to kube-system) must be explicitly allowed in egress rules or service discovery breaks entirely.

level-5-node-affinity.description: An ML workload pod is stuck in Pending and won't schedule anywhere. There are nodes available and no resource pressure — the scheduling constraint in the manifest itself is what prevents placement. The affinity rule references a node label that doesn't exist on any node in the cluster.
level-5-node-affinity.hint1: Inspect the pod's scheduling events: `kubectl describe pod gpu-workload -n k8smissions`
level-5-node-affinity.hint2: The events should say 'didn't match Pod's node affinity/selector'. Now check what labels your nodes actually carry: `kubectl get nodes --show-labels`. Compare those labels against what the affinity rule requires.
level-5-node-affinity.hint3: The `affinity.nodeAffinity.requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution.nodeSelectorTerms` block specifies the wrong label key and value. The key in the manifest doesn't match any label key on any node.
level-5-node-affinity.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod requires a node with label `gpu-type=nvidia-tesla`. The actual node has label `accelerator=gpu`. Neither the key (`gpu-type`) nor the value (`nvidia-tesla`) matches, so no node satisfies the required affinity and the pod stays Pending indefinitely.

## Fix

Update the affinity to match the actual node labels:

```yaml
affinity:
  nodeAffinity:
    requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      nodeSelectorTerms:
      - matchExpressions:
        - key: accelerator
          operator: In
          values:
          - gpu
```

## Why this matters

`requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` is a hard constraint: if no node matches, the pod will never schedule — it waits forever without error messages in the pod status itself. Always verify node labels with `kubectl get nodes --show-labels` before writing affinity rules. For non-critical placement preferences, use `preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` instead, which degrades gracefully.

level-6-taints-tolerations.description: A pod is stuck in Pending and cannot be scheduled anywhere. The node has capacity, the namespace quota is fine, and there is no affinity misconfiguration — but every node in the cluster has been reserved for a specific workload type using a taint. The pod has nothing to let it past that gate.
level-6-taints-tolerations.hint1: Read the pod's scheduling events: `kubectl describe pod regular-app -n k8smissions`
level-6-taints-tolerations.hint2: Look for 'node(s) had untolerated taint' in the events output. Then inspect the node's taints directly: `kubectl describe node kind-control-plane | grep -A5 Taints`
level-6-taints-tolerations.hint3: The pod spec has no `tolerations` block at all. Without a toleration that matches the node's taint key, value, and effect, the scheduler treats every tainted node as off-limits.
level-6-taints-tolerations.reveal:
## What went wrong

The node has the taint `dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule`. A `NoSchedule` taint means the scheduler will not place any pod on that node unless the pod explicitly tolerates it. The pod has no `tolerations` block, so it can't go anywhere.

## Fix

Add a toleration that matches the taint exactly:

```yaml
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "dedicated"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "gpu"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:latest
```

## Why this matters

Taints and tolerations are the mechanism node operators use to reserve nodes for specific workloads — GPU nodes, high-memory nodes, or nodes with special hardware. A toleration does not *require* the pod to land on that node; it merely *permits* it. Combine tolerations with `nodeAffinity` if you want to both allow and target a specific node type. The three taint effects behave differently: `NoSchedule` stops new pods, `PreferNoSchedule` is a soft hint, and `NoExecute` also evicts running pods.

level-7-poddisruptionbudget.description: A routine node maintenance procedure is failing. The operator runs `kubectl drain` but it errors immediately saying pod eviction would violate the disruption budget. The Deployment is running but its replica count and the PDB's minimum availability requirement are mathematically incompatible.
level-7-poddisruptionbudget.hint1: Inspect the PodDisruptionBudget for this workload: `kubectl describe pdb web-pdb -n k8smissions`
level-7-poddisruptionbudget.hint2: The PDB's Status section will show 0 'Allowed Disruptions', meaning zero pods can be evicted. Compare the PDB's `minAvailable` against the Deployment's actual replica count: `kubectl get deployment web-app -n k8smissions`
level-7-poddisruptionbudget.hint3: The PDB's `minAvailable` field is set higher than the Deployment's `replicas` count. Since the budget demands more running pods than exist, zero disruptions are ever permitted.
level-7-poddisruptionbudget.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Deployment has 2 replicas but the PDB says `minAvailable: 3`. Kubernetes can never satisfy this — you cannot have 3 available pods when only 2 exist. As a result, `AllowedDisruptions` is permanently 0 and any eviction (drain, rolling update, autoscaler) is blocked.

## Fix

Either increase replicas to 3 or lower `minAvailable` to a value the Deployment can satisfy:

```yaml
# Option A: keep replicas at 2, relax the PDB
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  minAvailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
```

Or increase replicas and keep `minAvailable: 2`:

```yaml
# In the Deployment:
spec:
  replicas: 3
```

## Why this matters

PodDisruptionBudgets protect availability during voluntary disruptions — cluster upgrades, node drains, and rolling updates all go through the eviction API. A PDB that can never be satisfied is worse than no PDB: it silently blocks all maintenance operations. The rule of thumb: `minAvailable` should always be strictly less than `replicas`, leaving at least one pod that can be disrupted.

level-8-pod-security.description: A pod is rejected before it even starts — there are no image pull errors and the YAML is syntactically valid, but the pod never appears in `kubectl get pods`. This namespace enforces strict Pod Security Standards, and the pod's security configuration violates several rules simultaneously.
level-8-pod-security.hint1: Check the events to see the admission error: `kubectl get events -n k8smissions --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -10`
level-8-pod-security.hint2: The event should reference 'pods restricted' and list the violations. Check what standard the namespace enforces: `kubectl get namespace k8smissions -o yaml | grep pod-security`. Then compare those requirements against the pod's current `securityContext`.
level-8-pod-security.hint3: The container's `securityContext` sets `runAsUser: 0` (root) and `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`. The namespace enforces the `restricted` Pod Security Standard, which forbids both and also requires dropping all capabilities and setting a seccomp profile.
level-8-pod-security.reveal:
## What went wrong

The namespace has the label `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted`. The restricted standard requires: non-root user, no privilege escalation, all capabilities dropped, and a seccomp profile. The pod violates all four.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:latest
    securityContext:
      runAsNonRoot: true
      runAsUser: 1000
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL
```

## Why this matters

Pod Security Standards (PSS) are built into Kubernetes since 1.25 and replace the deprecated PodSecurityPolicy. Three tiers exist: `privileged` (no restrictions), `baseline` (blocks known privilege escalations), and `restricted` (requires all the hardening above). The `enforce` label rejects non-compliant pods at admission; `warn` lets them through but shows a warning; `audit` logs them. The restricted profile aligns with CIS Kubernetes Benchmark recommendations for production workloads.

level-9-priorityclass.description: A critical API pod is stuck in Pending even though lower-priority development workloads are consuming the available CPU on the node. The scheduler cannot evict the dev pods to make room because there is no priority difference between them — no PriorityClass has been defined or assigned.
level-9-priorityclass.hint1: Check the critical pod's scheduling events: `kubectl describe pod critical-api -n k8smissions`
level-9-priorityclass.hint2: The events will say 'Insufficient cpu'. Now check the priority of all pods in the namespace: `kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,PRIORITY:.spec.priority`. Notice the critical pod has no priority set.
level-9-priorityclass.hint3: The critical pod spec has no `priorityClassName` field, and no PriorityClass resource exists in the cluster. Without a higher numeric priority, the scheduler has no basis for preempting the dev pods.
level-9-priorityclass.reveal:
## What went wrong

All pods in this namespace have the same default priority (0). The scheduler only preempts lower-priority pods when a higher-priority pod needs their resources. Without a PriorityClass and an assigned `priorityClassName`, every pod is equal and nothing gets evicted.

## Fix

Create PriorityClasses and assign them:

```yaml
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: low-priority
value: 100
globalDefault: false
description: "Development workloads"
---
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: high-priority
value: 1000
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority
globalDefault: false
description: "Critical production workloads"
```

Then set `priorityClassName: high-priority` on the critical pod and `priorityClassName: low-priority` on the dev pod.

## Why this matters

Pod priority is the mechanism Kubernetes uses to implement capacity reservations without fixed resource allocations. When the cluster is full and a high-priority pod arrives, the scheduler preempts (evicts) lower-priority pods to free space. This is how you guarantee critical services can always schedule, even under resource pressure. Never mix priority classes with equal values if you intend ordering — the numeric gap between classes is what determines preemption order.

level-10-chaos-finale.description: Production is down across the board. Multiple independent failures are active simultaneously: pods are stuck Pending, pods are being rejected at admission, RBAC errors fill the logs, and no traffic can flow. This is a compound incident — you must identify and fix each failure mode before the system stabilizes.
level-10-chaos-finale.hint1: Start with a broad scan before fixing anything: `kubectl get all -n k8smissions` and `kubectl get events -n k8smissions --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -20`. List every distinct failure type you observe before touching the manifest.
level-10-chaos-finale.hint2: You should find at least these failure modes: quota exceeded (replicas 3 × 1 CPU > quota of 1 CPU), admission rejection (runAsUser: 0 violates security policy), RBAC forbidden (app-sa has no Role), network blackout (deny-all NetworkPolicy with no rules), and PDB blocking evictions (minAvailable: 5 > replicas: 3). Fix quota first — nothing else can schedule until pods can start.
level-10-chaos-finale.hint3: The broken fields span multiple resources: `requests.cpu` is too high for the quota, `spec.replicas` exceeds what the quota can fit, `runAsUser: 0` violates the security policy, no Role or RoleBinding exists for `app-sa`, the NetworkPolicy has no rules, and `pdb.spec.minAvailable` is 5 for a 2-replica deployment.
level-10-chaos-finale.reveal:
## What went wrong

Six independent misconfigurations combined to take down the entire system:
1. Quota too restrictive: `requests.cpu: "1"` in the quota but pods each request 1 CPU with 3 replicas
2. Security violation: `runAsUser: 0` rejected by the namespace's restricted policy
3. Missing RBAC: `app-sa` has no Role or RoleBinding
4. Network blackout: `deny-all` NetworkPolicy with no ingress/egress rules
5. Impossible PDB: `minAvailable: 5` with only 2 replicas

## Fix

Fix each resource in order:

```yaml
# 1. Expand quota
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: chaos-quota
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: "2Gi"
    pods: "10"
---
# 2. Fix securityContext and reduce CPU requests in Deployment
# spec.replicas: 2  (fits quota: 2 × 200m = 400m < 2000m)
# containers[0].resources.requests.cpu: "200m"
# containers[0].securityContext:
#   runAsNonRoot: true
#   runAsUser: 1000
#   allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
#   capabilities:
#     drop: [ALL]
---
# 3. Add RBAC for app-sa
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: app-role
  namespace: k8smissions
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: app-binding
  namespace: k8smissions
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: app-role
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: app-sa
---
# 4. Replace deny-all with allow policy
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-app
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: chaos
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector: {}
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector: {}
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
    ports:
    - port: 53
      protocol: UDP
---
# 5. Fix PDB
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: chaos-pdb
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  minAvailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: chaos
```

## Why this matters

Real incidents are rarely single-cause. Compound failures compound each other: quota violations hide security violations, networking failures mask RBAC errors. The diagnostic discipline — enumerate all failures before fixing any one — prevents the fix for problem A from masking problem B. Triage order matters: fix scheduling blockers (quota, security) first so pods can start, then fix runtime issues (RBAC, network).

level-11-serviceaccount-rbac.description: A pod that reads ConfigMaps at startup to load application configuration is printing FORBIDDEN and exiting. The ServiceAccount it uses was created and assigned, but it was deployed without any RBAC permissions — making every API call it attempts a 403 error.
level-11-serviceaccount-rbac.hint1: Check the container's output to see the exact API response: `kubectl logs config-lister -n k8smissions`
level-11-serviceaccount-rbac.hint2: The logs should show 'FORBIDDEN' after `kubectl get configmaps`. Verify what RBAC is attached to the ServiceAccount: `kubectl get role,rolebinding -n k8smissions`. You should find nothing related to config-reader-sa.
level-11-serviceaccount-rbac.hint3: The `config-reader-sa` ServiceAccount has no Role and no RoleBinding. Kubernetes ServiceAccounts have zero API access by default — every permission must be explicitly granted.
level-11-serviceaccount-rbac.reveal:
## What went wrong

The ServiceAccount `config-reader-sa` exists but has no RBAC resources backing it. When the pod calls the Kubernetes API to list ConfigMaps, the API server checks the ServiceAccount's permissions, finds none, and returns HTTP 403 Forbidden.

## Fix

Create a Role scoped to configmaps and bind it to the ServiceAccount:

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: configmap-reader
  namespace: k8smissions
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: config-reader-binding
  namespace: k8smissions
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: configmap-reader
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: config-reader-sa
  namespace: k8smissions
```

## Why this matters

This is the same RBAC pattern as level 1, but applied to ConfigMaps instead of Pods. The lesson to reinforce: ServiceAccounts are identities, not permission grants. You can verify the fix before the pod even restarts with: `kubectl auth can-i list configmaps --as=system:serviceaccount:k8smissions:config-reader-sa -n k8smissions`. That command should return `yes` once the RoleBinding is applied.

level-12-non-root-enforcement.description: A pod is rejected at admission and never starts. The manifest has no explicit security settings — the container has no securityContext at all. Without one, the image runs as its default user, which is root, and this namespace's admission controller forbids root containers.
level-12-non-root-enforcement.hint1: Apply the manifest and read the admission error directly: `kubectl apply -f ~/.k8smissions/sandbox/manifest.yaml`. If already applied, check events: `kubectl get events -n k8smissions --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -5`
level-12-non-root-enforcement.hint2: The error should say 'container runs as root which is not allowed'. The fix does not require changing the image — you need to add a `securityContext` block to the pod spec that sets a non-root user.
level-12-non-root-enforcement.hint3: The pod spec has no `securityContext` block at all. The namespace policy requires `runAsNonRoot: true` and a non-zero `runAsUser`. Both must be added to make the pod compliant.
level-12-non-root-enforcement.reveal:
## What went wrong

Without a `securityContext`, the container runs as whatever user the image specifies. `busybox:1.36` defaults to UID 0 (root). The namespace's Pod Security admission controller checks this at creation time and rejects the pod.

## Fix

Add a securityContext at the pod level and harden the container:

```yaml
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox:1.36
    command:
    - /bin/sh
    - -c
    - id; sleep 3600
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
```

## Why this matters

The pod-level `securityContext` sets defaults for all containers. The container-level `securityContext` overrides or supplements those defaults. `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` adds defense-in-depth: even if an attacker achieves code execution, they cannot write malicious files to the container's filesystem. You can verify the running user with `kubectl exec insecure-pod -- id` after the fix — it should show `uid=1000`.

level-13-resourcequota-exceeded.description: New pods in this namespace fail to schedule with 'exceeded quota' errors even though the node has plenty of free CPU. The problem is not node capacity — one existing pod is consuming nearly all of the namespace's CPU quota allocation, leaving almost nothing for other workloads.
level-13-resourcequota-exceeded.hint1: Check how much of the namespace quota has been consumed: `kubectl describe resourcequota ns-quota -n k8smissions`
level-13-resourcequota-exceeded.hint2: The quota output will show `requests.cpu` near its hard limit. Now check which pod is responsible: `kubectl describe pod quota-buster -n k8smissions` and look at the `resources.requests` section in the spec.
level-13-resourcequota-exceeded.hint3: The `quota-buster` pod's `resources.requests.cpu` is set to 400m in a namespace with a 500m total CPU quota. That leaves only 100m for all other pods, making most scheduling requests fail.
level-13-resourcequota-exceeded.reveal:
## What went wrong

The namespace quota caps the total CPU requests at 500m. A single pod (`quota-buster`) claims 400m — 80% of the total. Any new pod requesting more than 100m CPU will be rejected at the API server with a quota exceeded error, even if nodes have gigabytes of free CPU capacity.

## Fix

Reduce the pod's resource requests to leave headroom for other pods:

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 100Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 200m
    memory: 200Mi
```

## Why this matters

ResourceQuota enforces *namespace-level* aggregate limits, not per-pod limits. A single over-requesting pod can block the entire namespace. The right tool for per-pod ceilings is a `LimitRange`, which sets default and maximum request values per container. Together, ResourceQuota and LimitRange form a two-layer governance model: the quota controls the namespace total, the LimitRange prevents any single container from claiming an unreasonable share of it.

level-14-capabilities.description: A network utility pod needs to listen on port 80, but it exits immediately with 'permission denied' when trying to bind to the socket. The pod correctly runs as a non-root user — but in dropping all Linux capabilities for security, it also dropped the one capability that non-root processes need to bind to ports below 1024.
level-14-capabilities.hint1: Check the container's exit output: `kubectl logs port-binder -n k8smissions`
level-14-capabilities.hint2: The logs should show 'FAILED to bind port 80'. Now inspect the securityContext: `kubectl get pod port-binder -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A15 securityContext`. You will see `capabilities.drop: [ALL]` with no corresponding `add` section.
level-14-capabilities.hint3: The `securityContext.capabilities` block drops ALL capabilities but has no `add:` section. The `NET_BIND_SERVICE` capability was dropped along with everything else, and without it a non-root process cannot bind to any port below 1024.
level-14-capabilities.reveal:
## What went wrong

Linux capabilities are fine-grained permissions that grant specific kernel-level privileges. `NET_BIND_SERVICE` is the one that allows binding to privileged ports (ports < 1024). When you drop ALL capabilities, you drop this one too. A non-root process without it gets `EACCES` (permission denied) when calling `bind(80)`.

## Fix

Drop everything and add back only what's needed:

```yaml
securityContext:
  runAsNonRoot: true
  runAsUser: 1000
  capabilities:
    drop:
    - ALL
    add:
    - NET_BIND_SERVICE
```

## Why this matters

This is the principle of least privilege applied to Linux capabilities. Rather than running as root (which implicitly has all capabilities), you: (1) drop all capabilities first, then (2) add back only the specific ones the process requires. This limits blast radius if the process is ever compromised — the attacker gets `NET_BIND_SERVICE` but not `SYS_ADMIN`, `CAP_NET_RAW`, `SYS_PTRACE`, and all the other dangerous capabilities. You can list available capabilities with `man 7 capabilities`.

level-15-clusterrole-aggregate.description: An operator's ClusterRole is supposed to automatically accumulate permissions from other ClusterRoles using Kubernetes' label-based aggregation mechanism. The source ClusterRole exists and has the correct rules, but the permissions never appear in the operator's role. The aggregation selector and the label on the source role don't match.
level-15-clusterrole-aggregate.hint1: Inspect the operator's ClusterRole to see if it has any rules: `kubectl get clusterrole operator-role -o yaml`
level-15-clusterrole-aggregate.hint2: The `rules:` section is empty despite the source ClusterRole existing. Compare what the `aggregationRule.clusterRoleSelectors` requires against the label on `deployments-reader`: `kubectl get clusterrole deployments-reader -o yaml | grep -A5 labels`
level-15-clusterrole-aggregate.hint3: The `deployments-reader` ClusterRole has the label `rbac.example.io/aggregate-to-viewer: 'true'`, but `operator-role`'s aggregationRule selects for `rbac.example.io/aggregate-to-operator: 'true'`. The label value string is wrong — it says `viewer` instead of `operator`.
level-15-clusterrole-aggregate.reveal:
## What went wrong

Kubernetes aggregated ClusterRoles work by continuously scanning all ClusterRoles for matching labels and merging their rules into the parent. `operator-role` looks for label `rbac.example.io/aggregate-to-operator: 'true'`. The source role has `aggregate-to-viewer` — one word off — so the selector never matches and the parent's `rules:` stays empty.

## Fix

Change the label on `deployments-reader` to match the selector:

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: deployments-reader
  labels:
    rbac.example.io/aggregate-to-operator: 'true'  # was: aggregate-to-viewer
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
```

Kubernetes will automatically detect the new label and merge the rules into `operator-role` within seconds — no restart required.

## Why this matters

ClusterRole aggregation is how Kubernetes itself builds the built-in `admin`, `edit`, and `view` roles — CRD controllers add permissions by labeling a ClusterRole with `rbac.authorization.k8s.io/aggregate-to-edit: 'true'`. This pattern lets you extend roles without modifying them directly, which is important in multi-tenant clusters where you don't own the parent role. The entire mechanism is label-matching, so a single character difference silently breaks it — always verify with `kubectl get clusterrole operator-role -o yaml` after applying labels to confirm the rules were merged.
